Interview transcript -- Ops Lead (Marcus), 10 Mar 2025, ~15 min, informal
chat over coffee about how returns actually work day to day. Lightly
cleaned up, filler words removed. Not verbatim.

Q: Walk me through what happens when a customer wants to return something.

A: Yeah so, honestly it's kind of split across three places right now,
which is annoying. Customer emails support, could be sarah@ or whoever's
on support that week, and support logs it -- we call that opening an RMA,
though "RMA sheet" is really just a row in our Returns Tracker spreadsheet.
We send them a prepaid label out of Shopify, well, actually the label
generation is manual too, someone just buys it through the carrier
website and forwards it. That part could really be automated but isn't
yet.

Q: Then what?

A: Item comes back to the warehouse, Priya or whoever's covering warehouse
that day inspects it. She's checking basically two things -- are the tags
still on, and does it look unworn/undamaged. If yes, it's approved, and
depending what the customer asked for on the original ticket we either
reship the right item or process a refund. If no -- tags missing, looks
worn, whatever -- it's denied, full stop, no refund and no reship. That's
policy, not really a judgment call, it's written up as our "final sale if
worn" rule and support's supposed to tell customers about it upfront but
half the time they don't remember to.

Q: What system records the decision?

A: Priya updates the Returns Tracker with "Approved" or "Denied" and what
the resolution was. The original order lives in Order Mgmt System though
-- that's our name for the Shopify backend, basically -- so if it's a
reship, whoever's on ops (usually me or Dave) has to go create a new order
in there by hand. That handoff is the annoying part, Priya can't do it
herself and it means reships sit for a day sometimes waiting on one of us.

Q: What about denied returns -- does the customer get told anything?

A: They're supposed to get an email explaining it was denied because the
item didn't pass inspection, per the final-sale policy. In practice I
think that email gets forgotten some of the time, honestly. It's not
automated, whoever's watching the tracker that day is meant to send it.
